name: Dokkaebi
description: Dokkaebi, also known as Korean goblins, are mischievous and powerful spirits that play a significant role in Korean folklore. Unlike western goblins, they are not inherently evil but are more akin to tricksters and sometimes even benevolent beings. Originating from inanimate objects possessed by spirits, particularly old or discarded household items, they are often associated with fire and blacksmithing. Dokkaebi possess supernatural powers, including shapeshifting, invisibility, and the ability to manipulate objects. They are known for their love of games, contests of strength, and practical jokes, often rewarding those who please them and punishing those who offend them. While their appearance varies, they are frequently depicted as grotesque, with large heads, horns, and fearsome teeth, though some are also portrayed as more human-like. They are considered guardians of sacred places and are often invoked for good fortune and protection against evil spirits. Their stories serve as moral lessons, highlighting the importance of humility, honesty, and respect. They are a complex figure in Korean mythology, representing both the playful and the potent aspects of the spirit world, and their influence continues to be felt in modern Korean culture through art, literature, and festivals.
subtitle: Mischievous Korean Goblin Spirits
appearance: Dokkaebi appearances vary greatly, but they are commonly depicted as having large, grotesque features, including oversized heads, horns, and sharp teeth. Their skin is often described as reddish or dark. Some are depicted with a single leg or eye. They often carry a magical club or cudgel called a 'dokkaebi gamtu' that grants wishes and enhances their powers. While some are monstrous, others appear more human-like, though always with a distinct and somewhat unsettling aura.
habitat: Dokkaebi are typically found in forests, mountains, abandoned buildings, and other remote locations. They are often associated with places where humans once lived or worked, such as old homes, blacksmith shops, and graveyards. They are also believed to inhabit sacred groves and protect them from intruders.
behavior: Dokkaebi are known for their mischievous behavior, playing pranks on humans, challenging them to games, and generally causing playful chaos. However, they are not inherently malicious and can be quite generous to those who treat them with respect and kindness. They enjoy contests of strength and cleverness, often rewarding winners and punishing losers. They are also fiercely protective of their territory and will defend it against intruders.

keyMyths.title: The Dokkaebi's Nose
keyMyths.description: A man tricks a dokkaebi into revealing the secret to becoming rich: trading his nose. The man then exploits the dokkaebi's generosity for personal gain, learning a lesson about greed.

keyMyths.title: The Dokkaebi and the Kind Woodcutter
keyMyths.description: A woodcutter shares his food with a dokkaebi and is rewarded with riches. The dokkaebi's generosity highlights the importance of kindness and compassion in Korean folklore.

keyMyths.title: The Dokkaebi's Magic Club
keyMyths.description: A dokkaebi uses its magic club to create riches and cause mischief. The story illustrates the power and unpredictability of these mythical creatures.
